Constitutional Usage

Founding-era documented usage — real quotes, every one verifiable.

The Usage Layer

The words, as they were used

1395 of 1397 constitutional words carry real founding-era usage.

What this is

Every quote here is a real occurrence pulled from the founding-era record — nothing generated. Pick a word to see up to three quotes. Each one names its source and opens to a fuller passage, so you can verify or read it in context, not just take it on faith.

The corpus these quotes come from

The Federalist Papers (1787-88)The Anti-Federalist PapersMadison, Notes of the Federal Convention (1787)Elliot's Debates, Vol. 1Elliot's Debates, Vol. 2Elliot's Debates, Vol. 3Elliot's Debates, Vol. 4Farrand's Records, Vol. 1Farrand's Records, Vol. 2Story, Commentaries on the Constitution, Vol. 1Rawle, A View of the Constitution

This is the second point of verification — documented usage at the time — built independently of any dictionary. Where a dictionary cell (Johnson and the rest) is still empty, this layer stands on its own.
